title: Whole Hog Order
triggers: whole hog|buy a whole hog|purchase a whole hog|full hog|entire hog|whole pig
response: A whole hog order means you get the full animal processed to your cut specifications. Typical hanging weight runs 180–220 lbs, and you take home roughly 130–160 lbs of finished cuts depending on your choices. Ask me for a full estimate.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|What cuts from a whole hog?

title: Half Hog Order
triggers: half hog|buy a half hog|purchase half a hog|split hog|half pig
response: A half hog splits the animal lengthwise. Typical hanging weight is 90–110 lbs, and you take home roughly 65–80 lbs of finished cuts. A good option if a whole hog is more than your family needs.
suggestions: Calculate a half hog|What cuts from a half hog?

title: How Much Meat from a Hog
triggers: how much meat from a hog|yield from a hog|take home weight|how many pounds|pork yield
response: A whole hog typically yields 130–160 lbs finished. A half hog yields 65–80 lbs. Exact amount depends on the animal's size and how you choose to have it cut. Ask me for a yield estimate based on typical farm weights.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|How much freezer space?

title: Whole vs Half Hog
triggers: whole hog vs half|should i get whole or half|difference between whole and half|is whole better than half
response: Whole hog is a better per-pound value and gives you more variety. Half hog is better if you have limited freezer space or want to try farm-raised pork before committing to a full animal. Both come with a custom cut sheet.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|Calculate a half hog

title: Family Size for Hog
triggers: how many people does a hog feed|feed a family|how long does it last|enough for family
response: A whole hog typically provides a family of four with several months of pork — chops, roasts, bacon, sausage, ribs, and more. A half hog is often enough for a smaller family or to supplement your regular grocery shopping.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|How much freezer space?

title: First Time Buying a Hog
triggers: never bought a whole hog|first time|new to buying farm meat|don't know where to start|first purchase
response: No worries — most people haven't done this before. Here's the short version: pick whole or half, we schedule processing, you fill out a cut sheet, and pick up your packaged meat. I can walk you through the estimate and what to expect step by step.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|What is a cut sheet?|Go to Products

title: What Cuts from a Hog
triggers: what cuts do i get|hog cuts|pork cuts|list of cuts|what comes from a hog
response: From a hog you typically get: pork chops, shoulder roasts, hams, bacon (belly), sausage, ribs, hocks, and soup bones. Exact cut breakdown depends on your cut sheet choices. Ask me for a full cut-by-cut estimate.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|Calculate a half hog

title: How Hogs Are Raised
triggers: how are the hogs raised|pasture raised pork|farm raised pork|how do you raise pigs
response: Our hogs are raised on the farm in Maitland, Missouri — not in a commercial facility. Farm-raised on pasture with good feed.
suggestions: Tell Me About the Farm|Go to Products

title: Fresh or Frozen Pork
triggers: is it fresh or frozen|how is it packaged|vacuum sealed|paper wrap|packaging
response: All processed meat is packaged and frozen at the processor. It arrives at pickup in good frozen condition, ready for your freezer. Packaging method depends on the processor.
suggestions: Go to Products|How much freezer space?

title: Pork Quality
triggers: how good is the pork|is it good quality|is farm pork better|quality of meat
response: Farm-raised pork from animals you can trace to a known farm is genuinely different from commodity grocery pork — better fat distribution, real flavor, and no mystery about how it was raised.

title: Whole Lamb Order
triggers: whole lamb|buy a whole lamb|purchase a whole lamb|full lamb|entire lamb
response: A whole lamb gives you the full carcass processed to your cut specs. Katahdin lambs typically hang at 45–65 lbs and yield about 30–44 lbs of finished cuts. I can run a full estimate.
suggestions: Calculate a whole lamb|What cuts from a lamb?

title: Half Lamb Order
triggers: half lamb|buy a half lamb|purchase half a lamb|split lamb
response: A half lamb is half the carcass. Typical hanging weight 22–32 lbs, take-home around 15–22 lbs. A good way to try lamb without committing to a whole animal.
suggestions: Calculate a half lamb|What cuts from a half lamb?

title: How Much Meat from a Lamb
triggers: how much meat from a lamb|lamb yield|take home from lamb|pounds of lamb
response: A whole Katahdin lamb typically yields 30–44 lbs of finished cuts. A half lamb yields 15–22 lbs. Exact yield depends on the animal's size and your cut choices.
suggestions: Calculate a whole lamb|How much freezer space for lamb?

title: How Does Lamb Taste
triggers: how does lamb taste|is lamb gamey|lamb flavor|does katahdin lamb taste strong|lamb vs beef
response: Katahdin lamb is known for its mild, clean flavor — noticeably less gamey than many traditional wool-breed lambs. The meat-focused genetics and Missouri grass diet produce a well-rounded flavor that surprises most first-timers.
suggestions: Try a whole lamb|Go to Products

title: First Time Buying Lamb
triggers: never bought lamb|first time with lamb|new to lamb|don't know lamb cuts
response: Lamb is simpler than people expect. A whole or half lamb comes with a cut sheet where you choose how you want it processed — chops, roasts, ground lamb, or shanks. I can walk you through what to expect.
suggestions: Calculate a whole lamb|What cuts from a lamb?

title: Whole vs Half Lamb
triggers: whole lamb vs half|should i get whole or half lamb|difference whole half lamb
response: Whole lamb gives better value per pound and more variety. Half lamb is great for first-time buyers or smaller families. Katahdin lambs are moderate-sized — a whole isn't overwhelming for a family of four.
suggestions: Calculate a whole lamb|Calculate a half lamb

title: Cuts from a Lamb
triggers: what cuts do i get from a lamb|lamb cuts list|what comes from a lamb|cuts from lamb
response: From a whole lamb: leg roasts, loin chops, shoulder, rack of lamb, shanks, ground lamb, stew meat, and soup bones. Specific options depend on your cut sheet choices and processor.
suggestions: Calculate a whole lamb|How much freezer space for lamb?

title: Katahdin Lamb Quality
triggers: katahdin lamb|is katahdin lamb good|why katahdin|quality of katahdin meat
response: Katahdin sheep are a meat-breed hair sheep — selected for muscle, yield, and carcass quality rather than wool. The result is a mild, well-muscled lamb with good fat coverage and less lanolin flavor than traditional breeds.
suggestions: Tell Me About Katahdins|Go to Products

title: Lamb vs Mutton
triggers: difference between lamb and mutton|what is mutton|lamb vs mutton
response: Lamb comes from animals under one year old (some define it as under two). Mutton comes from older sheep and has a stronger, more pronounced flavor. We sell lamb, not mutton.
suggestions: Go to Products|Tell Me About the Farm

title: Is Lamb Seasonal
triggers: is lamb available year round|seasonal lamb|when is lamb available
response: Lamb availability depends on our flock's production schedule. Check the Products page for current availability, or contact Dominic to ask about upcoming processing dates.

title: Freezer Space for Whole Hog
triggers: freezer space whole hog|how much room for whole hog|whole hog freezer|cubic feet whole hog
response: A whole hog typically needs 8–10 cubic feet of freezer space. That's roughly a 10-cubic-foot chest freezer, about half full. Exact space depends on cut choices and packaging.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|What kind of freezer?

title: Freezer Space for Half Hog
triggers: freezer space half hog|how much room for half hog|half hog freezer|cubic feet half hog
response: A half hog typically needs 4–5 cubic feet. That fits comfortably in a small chest freezer or the bottom of a standard upright.
suggestions: Calculate a half hog|What kind of freezer?

title: Freezer Space for Whole Lamb
triggers: freezer space whole lamb|how much room for lamb|whole lamb freezer|cubic feet lamb
response: A whole lamb typically needs about 2–3 cubic feet of freezer space. It fits easily alongside other items in a standard chest or upright freezer.

title: Freezer Space for Half Lamb
triggers: freezer space half lamb|half lamb freezer|cubic feet half lamb
response: A half lamb needs roughly 1–1.5 cubic feet. Very manageable — fits in any standard freezer alongside your existing groceries.

title: What Kind of Freezer
triggers: what kind of freezer|chest freezer or upright|best freezer|what freezer do i need
response: A chest freezer is the most efficient for bulk meat storage — better energy use and more usable space per cubic foot. Upright freezers work fine too. For a whole hog, a 10-cubic-foot chest freezer is a practical starting point.
suggestions: How much freezer space for whole hog?

title: How Long Does Frozen Meat Last
triggers: how long does meat last|shelf life|best by|how long in freezer|frozen meat storage
response: Properly frozen and packaged meat stays at best quality for 12–18 months. After that it's still safe but quality may decline. Date your packages and rotate stock first-in, first-out.

title: How Is It Packaged
triggers: how is it packaged|vacuum sealed|butcher paper|paper wrap|packaging type
response: Packaging depends on the processor. Most use vacuum seal or heavy butcher paper wrap. Both work well for freezer storage. Ask Dominic when you book your order for specific packaging options.

title: Stocking Up for the Year
triggers: stock up for the year|year supply|annual supply|buy for the year|family freezer plan
response: A whole hog and a whole lamb together give a family of four a well-rounded year's supply of meat — pork variety plus lamb for variety. Budget for a 10–12 cubic foot freezer minimum.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|Calculate a whole lamb

title: Prepare Your Freezer
triggers: prepare freezer|get freezer ready|before pickup|freezer checklist
response: Before pickup: make sure your freezer is running cold (0°F or below), defrost if needed, and clear enough space. Have a thermometer in the freezer to verify temperature. Meat should go in as soon as you get home.

title: What Is Custom Processing
triggers: what is custom processing|what does processing mean|how does it work
response: Custom processing means your animal goes to a licensed facility, is slaughtered and processed to your specifications, and returned to you packaged and ready for the freezer. You specify how you want it cut via a cut sheet.
suggestions: What is a cut sheet?|Who processes your animals?

title: What Is a Cut Sheet
triggers: what is a cut sheet|cut sheet|how do i fill out a cut sheet|cut instructions
response: A cut sheet is the form you give the processor telling them exactly how you want your animal cut — chop thickness, bone-in vs boneless, how much ground vs sausage, etc. I can walk you through what to expect for hog or lamb.
suggestions: What cuts come from a hog?|What cuts come from a lamb?

title: What Is Hanging Weight
triggers: hanging weight|what does hanging weight mean|carcass weight|what is hang weight
response: Hanging weight is the carcass weight after slaughter, before cutting and trimming. It's used to calculate your cost. Your final take-home weight will be less — roughly 65–75% of hanging weight depending on your cut choices.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|How is price calculated?

title: Take-Home vs Hanging Weight
triggers: take home weight|finished weight|packaged weight|how much do i actually get
response: Take-home (finished) weight is what ends up in your freezer after the processor trims, bones, and packages. For hog, expect roughly 65–72% of hanging weight. For lamb, roughly 65–68%. Exact percentage depends on cut choices.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|Calculate a whole lamb

title: Who Processes the Animals
triggers: who processes|where is it processed|which processor|processing facility|butcher shop
response: Our animals are processed at an inspected facility. Contact Dominic for the specific processor and facility details for your order.

response: We use inspected processing facilities for all farm meat sales. Contact Dominic for specifics on the facility used for your order.

title: Cooking Pork Chops
triggers: how to cook pork chops|pork chop recipe|grill pork chops|pan fry chops
response: Pork chops from a farm-raised hog are best grilled or pan-seared over high heat — 4–5 minutes per side for a 3/4-inch chop. Let them rest 3 minutes before cutting. Brining for an hour before cooking keeps them juicy.
suggestions: Go to Products|Calculate a whole hog

title: Cooking Pork Shoulder
triggers: pork shoulder recipe|how to cook shoulder|pulled pork|slow cook shoulder
response: Pork shoulder is built for low-and-slow. Smoke at 225°F for 8–10 hours, or braise in the oven at 300°F for 4–5 hours until it pulls apart easily. Salt and pepper plus smoke does most of the work.

title: Cooking Ham
triggers: how to cook ham|ham recipe|bake a ham|fresh ham|cured ham
response: Fresh (uncured) ham roasts like a large pork roast — 325°F until internal temp hits 160°F. Cured hams can be baked low-and-slow with a glaze. Your cut sheet choice determines which type you get.

title: Cooking Ribs
triggers: how to cook ribs|rib recipe|smoked ribs|pork ribs|bbq ribs
response: Low and slow is the only way. Smoke at 225°F for 4–5 hours (spare ribs) or 3–4 hours (baby backs) using the 3-2-1 method for tender results. Finish with a 10-minute glaze sear.

title: Cooking Bacon
triggers: how to cook bacon|bacon recipe|fry bacon|bake bacon|farm bacon
response: Farm bacon is thicker and meatier than grocery bacon. Cook low and slow in a cast iron pan for even rendering, or bake at 375°F on a rack for 20–25 minutes. The fat is worth saving.

title: Cooking Sausage
triggers: how to cook sausage|sausage recipe|fresh sausage|breakfast sausage|fry sausage
response: Fresh sausage is best pan-fried over medium heat until cooked through — 10–12 minutes, turning frequently. Don't rush it or the casing bursts. Links also grill well over indirect heat.

title: Cooking Lamb Chops
triggers: how to cook lamb chops|loin chops lamb|grill lamb chops|pan sear lamb chops
response: Loin chops and rib chops grill beautifully — high heat, 3–4 minutes per side for medium-rare. Season simply: salt, pepper, garlic, rosemary. Rest 5 minutes before serving.

title: Cooking Lamb Roast
triggers: how to cook lamb roast|leg of lamb|roast lamb|lamb leg recipe
response: Leg of lamb roasts at 325°F — about 20 minutes per pound for medium (145°F internal). Stud with garlic cloves, rub with olive oil, salt, pepper, and rosemary. Let it rest 15 minutes before carving.

title: Cooking Lamb Shanks
triggers: how to cook lamb shanks|lamb shank recipe|braise lamb shanks|slow cook shanks
response: Lamb shanks are best braised — brown first in a Dutch oven, then cook low and slow in stock or wine at 325°F for 2.5–3 hours until the meat falls off the bone. Rich and deeply flavorful.

title: Cooking Ground Meat
triggers: how to cook ground lamb|ground pork|ground meat recipe|use ground lamb|use ground pork
response: Ground lamb and pork are incredibly versatile — burgers, meatballs, pasta sauce, shepherd's pie, meatloaf. Farm-raised ground meat has better fat content and flavor than commercial.
suggestions: Calculate a whole lamb|Calculate a whole hog

title: Rack of Lamb
triggers: rack of lamb|how to cook rack of lamb|lamb rack recipe|roast rack
response: Rack of lamb is a showstopper — French the bones, sear in a hot pan, coat with a herb crust, then roast at 400°F for 15–20 minutes to medium-rare (130°F internal). Rest 5 minutes, carve between bones.

title: Cooking Hocks
triggers: pork hocks|how to cook hocks|ham hocks|smoked hocks
response: Hocks are collagen-rich — perfect for low-and-slow braises, bean soups, or collard greens. Smoke them first if you want extra depth. Long, slow cooking is the only way to unlock their flavor.

title: Why Buy Local Meat
triggers: why buy local|local farm meat|why not grocery store|why support local farm|local vs store
response: Local farm meat is traceable, raised by someone you can call by name, processed at a known facility, and genuinely different from commodity product. You're getting real food and supporting a Missouri family farm.
suggestions: Tell Me About the Farm|Go to Products

title: Farm vs Grocery Store
triggers: farm vs grocery|how different from store|better than grocery|farm raised vs commercial
response: Grocery store pork and lamb is typically commodity product — raised in bulk, processed at large facilities, often shipped a long way. Shiloh Ridge meat comes from animals raised on one farm, processed locally, and sold directly to you.

title: What Farm Raised Means
triggers: what does farm raised mean|farm to table|direct from farm|traceable meat
response: Farm-raised here means: raised at Shiloh Ridge Farm in Maitland, Missouri, by Dominic Hanway — not a USDA category or marketing label. You can see where it came from, who raised it, and how it was cared for.
suggestions: Tell Me About the Farm|Go to Products

title: Stocking Multiple Animals
triggers: buying hog and lamb|multiple animals|order both|more than one animal|combo order
response: Buying a whole hog and whole lamb together is a great combination — pork variety plus lamb for a change of pace. Plan for 10–13 cubic feet of freezer space total. Budget-wise, it's a full year of protein for most families.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|Calculate a whole lamb

title: Whole Hog Value
triggers: is whole hog worth it|value of whole hog|cost effective|best bang for buck|worth buying whole
response: Whole hog is the best per-pound value of any order size. You get variety (chops, roasts, bacon, ribs, sausage, hams), plus you control how every cut is done. For a family with freezer space, it's hard to beat.
suggestions: Calculate a whole hog|How much freezer space?
